title: Universality in numerical computations with random data. Analytical results.
speaker: Percy Deift
abstract: This is joint work with Tom Trogdon. Here the author shows how to prove universality rigorously for certain numerical algorithms of the type described in the first lecture. The proofs rely on recent state of the art results from random matrix theory.

proof_stages.label: Empirical universality question for randomized computations
proof_stages.summary: The talk begins from numerical evidence that normalized stopping times for matrix algorithms with random input have ensemble-independent fluctuation laws within suitable scaling regimes, motivating a rigorous theorem.

proof_stages.label: Toda largest-eigenvalue stopping problem
proof_stages.summary: The speaker selects the Toda flow as a nontrivial eigenvalue algorithm, defines its isospectral matrix evolution and the first-row off-diagonal stopping time t_1, and explains that this stopping time computes the largest eigenvalue to accuracy epsilon.

proof_stages.label: Gap-law theorem in the edge scaling regime
proof_stages.summary: The main theorem states that, for invariant and Wigner ensembles in the regime log(epsilon^{-1})/log n > 5/3 + sigma/2, the normalized Toda stopping time converges to the universal distribution of the inverse top eigenvalue gap.

proof_stages.label: Random-matrix hypotheses and good events
proof_stages.summary: The proof framework specifies generalized Wigner and invariant ensembles, equilibrium-measure constants and quantiles, and the high-probability rigidity, gap, and eigenvector-component conditions needed for the later estimates.

proof_stages.label: Spectral formula from Toda integrability
proof_stages.summary: Moser's explicit Toda formulas express the evolving first eigenvector components, x_11(t), and the stopping quantity E(t) entirely in terms of eigenvalues and first eigenvector components.

proof_stages.label: Deterministic localization of the stopping time
proof_stages.summary: On the good set of matrices, E(t) is decomposed into positive pieces, the dominant piece is shown to be monotone, and a deterministic reference time t_* is proved to approximate the actual stopping time t_1.

proof_stages.label: Probabilistic reduction to the top eigenvalue gap
proof_stages.summary: High-probability good-event estimates turn the deterministic comparison into t_1≈t_*, then t_* is replaced by an explicit t-hat depending only on the top eigenvalue gap, yielding the stated universal limiting law and highlighting further random-matrix inputs needed for stronger stopping rules.

examples.name: QR and Toda histograms for Bernoulli versus GOE data
examples.note: The plotted finite-size stopping-time histograms show that a fixed algorithm gives the same fluctuation law for different input ensembles, while QR and Toda give different laws.

examples.name: Same spectrum with different eigenvalue ordering
examples.note: Two matrices with the same eigenvalues but different ordering under the sorting flow illustrate why one might expect eigenvector information to affect the runtime, making its disappearance in the theorem noteworthy.
examples.engagement_reason: he reasons through two displayed matrices with the same spectrum but different eigenvalue ordering to explain why Toda sorting should make one converge faster than the other

passes.reason: The isospectral pair with different eigenvalue ordering is used as an adversarial illustration that a deterministic spectral-only account of stopping time could fail.
The same-spectrum but differently ordered inputs are extreme favorable/adversarial orientations for the sorting flow, showing naive spectral-only deterministic behavior can fail.
Sorted versus reverse-ordered isospectral inputs are extreme cases for a sorting flow and serve as an enemy case to spectrum-only intuition.
Permuted same-spectrum, nearly diagonal ordered-versus-reversed cases are best/worst sorting configurations used to challenge spectrum-only dependence.
The same-spectrum matrices with opposite eigenvalue ordering are best/worst ordering cases used as an adversarial illustration against a naive spectrum-only timing rule.
The same-spectrum matrices with opposite eigenvalue ordering are presented as an adversarial best-versus-worst sorting situation challenging a purely spectral explanation.
The same-spectrum but differently ordered nearly diagonal matrices are best/reverse-sorted boundary cases used to show that runtime need not be determined by spectrum alone.
The same-spectrum differently ordered matrices form sorted versus reverse-sorted enemy cases showing why runtime need not be determined by the spectrum alone.
Permuted nearly diagonal matrices with the same spectrum are used as an enemy case showing that spectral data alone need not determine runtime.
The differently ordered same-spectrum matrices form a nearly diagonal best-versus-worst sorting setup that obstructs the naive claim that spectrum alone fixes convergence time.

examples.name: Finite-n GUE/Bernoulli and GOE/BOE comparisons with the gap law
